subject.description: Marian legends, Dorothy, and Jerome, pp. 320-360

reviewNotes: The immediate edition is Anglia 3 (1880), not Anglia 8. Its article is signed by Horstmann at Munster on printed p. 360.

Horstmann's p. 320 prose says MS Lambeth 402, but the section headings repeatedly say 432 and the current Lambeth MS 432 catalog exactly matches the three contents; MS 402 is unrelated. The lineage resolves the witness to MS 432 while preserving the error.
Horstmann labels the Marian collection 'fol. 95'; Lambeth's current content record places it at f. 85. The discrepancy is retained as an open locator question.

On printed pp. 314-316, the Caxton Latin office continues in the lower page area while the Mirk addendum begins above it. The XML serializes both streams and therefore repeats PB references 314-316; this is not accidental duplicate pagination.

The Caxton unit in MS 306 is a bound early print, not a manuscript witness. It is textually incomplete because its final leaf is missing even though the cached surrogate completely covers the provider's current 415-canvas physical object.
The former Earl of Dysart copy is now Morgan PML 35083. Morgan records purchase through Quaritch from Sotheby's Ham House sale, lot 319, 20 June 1938.

lineage.summary: The General Corpus XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan CME item CME00061, which encodes Carl Horstmann's complete article 'Prosalegenden' from Anglia 3 (1880), pp. 293-360. Horstmann edited a heterogeneous selection: William Caxton's 1484 Life and Translation of Saint Winifred from the imperfect early print bound in Lambeth Palace Library MS 306, collated against the British Museum copy now BL C.10.b.19; John Mirk's Winifred sermon from BL Cotton Claudius A II with three short supplies or readings from Gonville and Caius MS 168/89; and Marian legends, a Life of Saint Dorothy, and a Life and Miracles of Saint Jerome from Lambeth MS 432. Complete whole-object caches retain all 618 canvases of BSB's Anglia 3 volume and all 415 canvases of Lambeth MS 306, mapping the article and bound Caxton print inside them. No public whole-object facsimile was found for MS 432, Cotton Claudius A II, Caius MS 168/89, or Horstmann's BL Caxton collation copy; mediated requests are therefore directed to the complete physical objects rather than cropped work portions.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous and composite medieval authorship; William Caxton and John Mirk have scoped responsibilities
catalogSummary.date: Medieval components chiefly attested in the fifteenth century; Caxton print 1484; editorial selection published 1880
catalogSummary.region: England and Welsh saint traditions; edited and published at Halle an der Saale, Germany
catalogSummary.title: Prosalegenden

catalogingSubject.label: Prosalegenden
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Carl Horstmann's 1880 editorial selection as represented in CME00061: German introductions and apparatus framing Caxton's Life and Translation of Saint Winifred, a John Mirk Winifred sermon, fifteen Marian miracles, a Life of Saint Dorothy, and a nineteen-chapter Life and Miracles of Saint Jerome. The record does not collapse those separately transmitted medieval works, their physical witnesses and early-print copies, the Anglia article, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML into one medieval compilation.

contentParts.label: Horstmann's introduction to Caxton's Winifred
contentParts.summary: German discussion of Winifred's cult, Mirk's Festial, possible Latin sources, surviving Caxton copies, and transcription practice.

contentParts.label: Caxton's Life and Translation of Saint Winifred
contentParts.summary: The 1484 Caxton prose life, relic translation, Latin office, and Horstmann's appended Mirk sermon.

contentParts.label: Life of Saint Winifred

contentParts.summary: Winifred's vow of virginity, decapitation by Cradoc, restoration, holy well, religious life, and death.

contentParts.label: Translation of Saint Winifred and Latin office
notes: Its lower-page Latin office overlaps Mirk's upper-page addendum on printed pp. 314-316.
contentParts.summary: The transfer of Winifred's relics to Shrewsbury, shrine miracles, Caxton's explicit, and associated Latin liturgical material.

contentParts.label: John Mirk's sermon for Saint Winifred

locators.value: begins f. 80v; exact terminal folio unresolved

contentParts.summary: A Festial sermon retelling Winifred's life, translation to Shrewsbury, and miracles for a preaching audience.

contentParts.label: Horstmann's introduction to the MS 432 texts
contentParts.summary: German source description and outline of the Jerome cycle, with an erroneous reference to MS 402.

contentParts.label: Fifteen Marian legends

locators.value: starts f. 85; inferred through f. 89v before Dorothy at f. 90

notes: Horstmann's heading says f. 95, conflicting with Lambeth's current f. 85 content record.
contentParts.summary: Fifteen brief miracles centered on Ave Maria devotion, Marian aid, rescue, healing, intercession, and visionary reward.

contentParts.label: Life of Saint Dorothy
contentParts.summary: Dorothy's virginity, tortures, martyrdom, winter roses and apples, Theophilus's conversion, and a Latin prayer ending quod Fuller.

contentParts.label: Life and Miracles of Saint Jerome
contentParts.summary: An introduction and nineteen chapters drawing on the Golden Legend, pseudo-Augustinian and pseudo-Cyrillian letters, visions of the afterlife, miracles, and Birgittine revelations.

dateStatements.display: Published as Carl Horstmann's Prosalegenden in Anglia 3 (1880)
Printed by William Caxton at Westminster in 1484; the translation and abridgment existed by that date

assertion.notes: The normalized range is deliberately broad and does not equate witness date with exact composition.

dateStatements.display: Composed by the early fifteenth century; the direct Cotton witness is dated 1425-1449
The selected Marian, Dorothy, and Jerome texts are attested in fifteenth-century Lambeth MS 432
notes: This dates the witness, not necessarily the composition of each underlying legend.

extent.description: Four Roman-numbered hagiographic groupings inside six DIV2 elements, with 23 nested DIV3 parts (29 structural divisions total). The XML also contains 104 prose paragraphs, one two-line verse group, 71 page-break elements, 560 milestones, and 126 notes.

formStatements.description: All principal cataloged medieval items are prose. Two encoded Latin verse lines in one LG occur within embedded devotional apparatus and do not make the edited selection mixed-form.

scope.description: Horstmann's introductions, source notes, and footnote apparatus
Caxton office, prayers, rubrics, quoted sources, and manuscript devotional supplements

notes: The repository viewer should present Prosalegenden as an edited selection and expose the component labels and scoped lineages rather than showing one undifferentiated author/date.

Downloaded source presence is recorded in the lineage manifest. BSB Anglia 3 and Lambeth MS 306 caches are complete whole-provider objects; no public complete surrogate was found for the other three manuscript witnesses.
Italic letters in CME00061 chiefly encode Horstmann's expansion/supply typography, not photographed manuscript or incunable abbreviation shapes.

subjects.label: Saint Winifred: virginity, martyrdom, restoration, and cult
Translation of relics and shrine devotion
Pilgrimage, holy wells, shrines, and miraculous healing
Virgin martyrdom, chastity, persecution, and conversion
Saint Jerome, visions, purgatory, hell, and posthumous miracles
Transcription, abbreviation expansion, and witness collation

summaries.value: A hagiographic selection centered on saints, miracles, and devotional practice. Caxton's Winifred narrative recounts the Welsh virgin's attempted sexual coercion, decapitation, miraculous restoration, religious life, death, and the translation of her relics to Shrewsbury; Mirk retells the life and shrine miracles as a festal sermon. Fifteen Marian exempla emphasize prayer and intercession. Dorothy's life narrates virgin martyrdom and the winter roses and apples sent to Theophilus. The Jerome cycle combines a life, pseudo-epistolary visions, descriptions of the afterlife, and posthumous miracles.

The XML has six top-level divisions: Horstmann's Winifred preface; the Caxton section with Life, Translation, and Mirk addendum; a bridging commentary; Marian legends; Dorothy; and Jerome. Jerome contains an introduction and nineteen numbered chapters. The source pages 314-316 contain two physically simultaneous streams: the lower continuation of Caxton's Latin office and the upper beginning of Mirk's sermon.

The title Prosalegenden and the four Roman-numbered groupings belong to Horstmann's 1880 article. Dates, places, authorship, and source witnesses are therefore scoped to components. The collection is cataloged as prose despite a two-line Latin verse group and verse-like prayers embedded in apparatus. Horstmann's isolated MS 402 and Marian fol. 95 statements conflict with exact MS 432 content evidence and are retained as errors/open questions.

notes: An editorial article title, not a title for one medieval work or medieval compilation.

titles.value: Caxton's Ausgabe der Legende von S. Wenefreda
Here begynneth the lyf of the holy & blessid vyrgyn saynt Wenefryde

work.summary: A hagiographic selection centered on saints, miracles, and devotional practice. Caxton's Winifred narrative recounts the Welsh virgin's attempted sexual coercion, decapitation, miraculous restoration, religious life, death, and the translation of her relics to Shrewsbury; Mirk retells the life and shrine miracles as a festal sermon. Fifteen Marian exempla emphasize prayer and intercession. Dorothy's life narrates virgin martyrdom and the winter roses and apples sent to Theophilus. The Jerome cycle combines a life, pseudo-epistolary visions, descriptions of the afterlife, and posthumous miracles.
